fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

GILL J

These proceedings involve an enforcement application filed by the husband seeking that the wife make further payments to him to discharge her obligations under the orders that finalise the property proceedings.
The wife opposes and disputes any obligation to pay the husband further monies beyond what she has already paid. In doing so she seeks a correction of an order pursuant to the slip rule and further seeks the offsetting of any liability that she may have under the primary orders against an indemnification requirement imposed upon the husband as a condition of him being granted a stay, pending an ultimately unsuccessful appeal.
By primary judgment of 17 May 2024 orders were made (“the primary orders”), relevantly, to divide non-superannuation interests of the parties 67.5 to 32.5 in favour of the wife, but to do so in accordance with particular machinery provisions related to the sale of a property in Suburb G in the Australian Capital Territory (see paragraph 382 and following of the primary judgment). The operative provisions to give effect to this division were Orders 12 and 13 that provided for the husband to receive 0.325 ($717,650 + net proceeds Suburb G) - a certain amount (this certain amount was the matter the subject of the slip rule application by the wife) and the orders further provided as to the manner of calculation of the net proceeds. The figure of $717,650 was the assessment within the judgment of property excluding superannuation and the Suburb G property.
A further judgment regarding enforcement and mechanisms in respect of the sale of the property at Suburb G was delivered on 8 May 2025. That judgment amended Order 19 of the primary orders in order to incorporate further expenses associated with the sale, consequences of failures to pay mortgage obligations, and in relation to a costs award that was made in favour of the wife in the appeal proceedings (“the enforcement orders”).
Before embarking on an analysis of the application of the orders, it is necessary to consider that the wife seeks a slip rule amendment to the orders to change the amount deducted at Order 12 from $3,250 to $7,250.
Rule 10.13(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 permits a court at any time, to vary or set aside an order if there is an error arising in the order from an accidental slip or omission, or an order does not reflect the intention of the court.
At paragraphs 356 and 384 of the judgment the amounts referred to by the wife are identified. They relate to a motor vehicle forming part of the pool held by the husband at a value of $5,250 and a rates debt, identified by the judgment as owed by the husband of approximately $2,000. In calculating, under circumstances where the husband would retain that rates debt net property of $3,250, being the value of the motor vehicle reduced by the liability held by the father, the sum of $3,250 formed part of the equation.
Although the wife says the amount of $2,000 should have been added to the value of the car rather than deducted from it, leaving an amount of $7,250 such would be inconsistent with the facts as identified in the judgment and as also set out in the balance sheet contained within the judgment. The wife contends that the husband has not met the debt. The evidence does not establish such.
In any event there is no slip to be corrected and the amount in the formula should remain as $3,250.
The application to amend the orders is refused.
The wife also seeks to offset against any indebtedness payments to be made by the husband pursuant to an indemnity imposed upon him by orders of 4 September 2024, as a condition of a stay in respect of the sale of the Suburb G property pending appeal. That issue is dealt with later.
The wife further sets out a number of matters that she asserts that the husband should now bear responsibility for, including further garden costs and the accruing of further rates while the husband was in occupation of the premises, but which are not provided for by the orders. It is, however, the orders that determine the liability and entitlements of both the husband and the wife. They are not amenable to a recasting.

CALCULATION OF NET PROCEEDS

The calculation of the net proceeds of the Suburb G sale is set out by a combination of Order 13 of the primary orders and Order 19 as amended by the enforcement orders.
The wife raised a contest about a number of the deductions set out in the orders being for expenses that should be personally the responsibility of the husband. In particular, she pointed to the payment in respect of costs relating to the Full Court.
However, despite any general concession made by the husband in oral argument, in the absence of agreement between the parties to compromise the amount owing, the amount remains to be calculated in accordance with the orders. That means following the mechanisms set out at Order 13 and Order 19.
Order 19 requires the deduction from the sale price of the following:
(1) The amount to discharge the mortgage;

(2) Agents’ commissions and expenses;

(3) Legal costs and outlays for the sale; and

(4) A sum of $161,000 to be paid to the second respondent.

Further, by operation of Order 19(f)(iv) the deduction of the following amounts is required:
Payment to the mother:
The sum of $17,000 plus interest calculated in accordance with Rule 10.17 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 in relation to the costs awarded following the father’s unsuccessful appeal;
The sum of $550 for the repair of the roof;
The sum of $1,150 for garden maintenance following the father vacating the property;
The reimbursement of sums paid by the mother to the agent in advance of the sale;
A sum equal to 67.5 per cent of any increase in the loan indebtedness for the [Suburb G] property for the period 4 September 2024 until 21 November 2024;
A sum equal to the amount by which the wife’s payments for the mortgage for the [Suburb G] property for the period 21 November until the date of the settlement of the sale of that property exceed those of the husband;
Of those unquantified figures, the total figure for A. inclusive of interest is, as shown by the calculations of the conveyancer as provided by the wife in Exhibit W1, the amount of $18,133.55. D. as shown by the conveyancer is $6,147.00, E. as set out below is $1,957.69 and F. is nil.
The adjusted settlement statement and settlement directions show that after adjustments, legal fees, expenses, discharge of the mortgage and payment to the second respondent, a net figure of $221,275.81 was retained in the solicitor’s trust account.
To that figure may be added the deposit of $47,250 from which the commission for the real estate agent of $14,175 was deducted leaving an amount of $33,075 held from the deposit.
Together, this leaves a total of net proceeds, prior to consideration of the matters contained at Order 19(f)(iv), of $254,350.81.
The wife is then entitled to deduct from that amount those items identified at Order 19(f)(iv) totalling $27,938.24.
The consequence is that the net proceeds should be calculated as an amount of $254,350.81 - $27,938.24 leaving a balance of $226,412.57.
The amount then provided by Order 12 is 0.325 ($717,650 plus $226,412.57) minus $3,250, which equals $303,570.34.

OFFSETTING INDEMNITY

Order 1 of the orders of 4 September 2024 is in the following terms:
Order 18 of the orders of 17 May 2024 is stayed pending further order of the court, or resolution of the appeal, on condition that [Mr Thatcher] is to indemnify [Ms Thatcher] in relation to any interest accruing, or charges accruing, or fees imposed in respect of the loan related to [Suburb G] property from 10 July 2024 until the disposal of the appeal and, if unsuccessful, in relation to the property aspects of the appeal, until his compliance with Order 18.
The wife filed material to identify an amount for which she should be indemnified in respect of that order. The husband filed material that, while it included matters related to the indemnity, went beyond the scope of what had been directed.
The parties differed as to the interest relating to the indemnification period.
The wife identified that the husband did not vacate the property until 21 November 2024 (vacation of the property, in accordance with Order 18 of the orders of 17 May 2024 being the end point of the indemnification period).
The wife provided the calculation of interest received from the conveyancer and further statements from the bank. Interest from 10 July 2024 to 1 November 2024 was calculated at $11,299.46. The interest was calculated by the conveyancer until 21 November as accumulating a further $2,011.45. This results in total interest for the period of $13,310.91.
The husband identified interest accruing from 1 July 2024 until 31 October 2024 of $12,296.04, the appeal being disposed of on 1 November 2024. This was more than was calculated by the conveyancer for the period up until 1 November 2024 (presumably by reason of this calculation of interest commencing prior to the indemnification period), but less than until he vacated the property on 21 November 2024.
The husband identified that the amount paid by him for that period was $8,520. On his calculation he asserts that the shortfall of $3,776.04 was the amount indemnified by the original order of 4 September 2024.
Against the conveyancer’s calculation of interest during the relevant period the shortfall is $4,790.91.
He, however, claims that the indemnity was replaced by Order 19(f)(iv)E. of 8 May 2025. That does not accurately represent the effect of Order 19(f)(iv)E., as that order does not displace the indemnity order of 4 September 2024 insofar as it operated from 10 July 2024 until the period dealt with by Order 19(f)(iv)E., which commenced on 4 September 2024 and continued until 21 November 2024. From 4 September 2024, Order 19(f)(iv)E. displaces the earlier indemnity, but not before.
The appropriate calculation of the operation of the orders of 4 September 2024 and 8 May 2025 is for a complete indemnity for increase in the loan for the period of 10 July 2024 to 4 September 2024, and then 67.5 per cent of the increase in indebtedness for the period 4 September 2024 to 21 November 2024.
The interest that accrued during that first period is set out at Appendix 3 of the conveyancer’s calculations in Exhibit W1, of $2,092.82 for the period 10 July 2024 to 31 July 2024, $3,097.27 for August 2024, and then $3,003.97 for September 2024. An apportionment of the interest of the first three of 30 days of September applies one tenth of the total September interest to that period of $300.40. The total interest for that period is $5490.49.
This was met by the husband’s payments of interest, and no further liability accrues to him for that period from 10 July 2024 until 4 September 2024. Accordingly, the relevant remaining period for offsetting is that from 4 September 2024 until the husband vacated the property on 21 November 2024, for which he is liable to reimburse the wife 67.5 percent.
The conveyancer then calculated for the period 4 September 2024 to 21 November 2024 an increase in indebtedness of $2,900.29, 67.5 per cent of which is $1,957.69.
This represents the amount for which the husband remains liable pursuant to Order 19(f)(iv)E., which is less than he would have been liable for had the original indemnity remained undisplaced.
Deducting this from the $303,570.34 that the husband is otherwise entitled to by operation of Order 12 leaves a balance of $301,612.65.

The wife’s payments to date

The husband initially received, from the proceeds of the sale, an amount of $226,671.87.
On 25 September 2025 the wife paid a further amount of $43,346.06 to the husband.
In total he has received $270,017.93.
Pursuant to Order 12 the husband is entitled to receive a further $31,594.72 from the wife.

CONCLUSION

Orders will be made that provide for the wife to pay to the husband the sum of $31,594.72.
The wife sought to pay any remaining amount within 60 days to facilitate refinancing. The husband sought the amount be paid within 28 days. In order to avoid further applications becoming necessary, the longer period will be allowed. The parties’ orders sought are otherwise to be dismissed.
